title: Free Tip Calculator - Calculate Tips & Split Bills | gab.ae
description: Calculate tips instantly with our free tip calculator. Split bills, add custom tip percentages, and get total amounts. Perfect for restaurants and services.

config.formula: var tipAmount = billAmount * (tipPercent / 100); var totalBill = billAmount + tipAmount; var perPerson = totalBill / people;

content.heading: How to Use the Tip Calculator
content.body: Enter your bill amount, select your desired tip percentage (15-20% is standard in most countries), and specify how many people are splitting the bill. Our calculator instantly shows the tip amount, total bill, and cost per person. This tool helps ensure fair tipping and accurate bill splitting for restaurants, delivery, taxis, and other services.

content.heading: Tipping Guidelines by Service
content.body: Restaurant servers typically receive 15-20% for good service, 18% being the modern standard. Delivery drivers deserve 15-20% with a $3 minimum. Hair stylists and barbers usually get 15-20%, while taxi drivers receive 10-15%. Hotel housekeeping merits $2-5 per night, and bartenders get $1-2 per drink or 15-20% of the tab.

faqs.q: What's the standard tip percentage?
faqs.a: 18-20% is now standard for restaurants in the US, 15% for basic service, and up to 25% for exceptional service.

faqs.q: Do I tip on the pre-tax or total amount?
faqs.a: Tip on the pre-tax amount (subtotal) rather than the total with tax included.

faqs.q: Should I tip for takeout orders?
faqs.a: 10-15% is appropriate for takeout, as staff still prepare and package your order.

faqs.q: How do I split tips when paying separately?
faqs.a: Calculate the tip on the total bill, then divide by the number of people for fairness.

title: Car Payment Calculator - Auto Loan Calculator | gab.ae
description: Calculate monthly car payments with our auto loan calculator. Enter loan amount, interest rate, and term to see monthly payments and total interest costs.

config.formula: var monthlyRate = (interestRate / 100) / 12; var monthlyPayment = monthlyRate > 0 ? (loanAmount * monthlyRate * Math.pow(1 + monthlyRate, loanTerm)) / (Math.pow(1 + monthlyRate, loanTerm) - 1) : loanAmount / loanTerm; var totalCost = monthlyPayment * loanTerm; var totalInterest = totalCost - loanAmount;

content.heading: Understanding Auto Loan Calculations
content.body: Car payments depend on three main factors: loan amount (principal), interest rate (APR), and loan term (months). A longer term means lower monthly payments but higher total interest. A $25,000 car loan at 6.5% APR costs $489/month over 60 months but only $388/month over 72 months - though you'll pay $2,736 more in total interest with the longer term.

content.heading: Tips to Lower Your Car Payment
content.body: Make a larger down payment to reduce the loan amount. Shop around for the best interest rates - credit unions often offer rates 1-2% lower than traditional banks. Consider certified pre-owned vehicles that have already absorbed significant depreciation. Avoid extended warranties and add-ons that inflate the loan amount unnecessarily.

faqs.q: What's a good interest rate for an auto loan?
faqs.a: Excellent credit (720+) typically qualifies for 4-6% APR, good credit (640-719) sees 6-9%, while fair credit (580-639) may pay 9-15%.

faqs.q: Should I choose a longer loan term for lower payments?
faqs.a: Longer terms reduce monthly payments but increase total interest. 60 months is typically the sweet spot balancing affordability and total cost.

faqs.q: How much should I put down on a car?
faqs.a: Aim for 10-20% down on new cars, 10% on used cars to avoid being underwater on the loan and reduce monthly payments.

faqs.q: Can I get pre-approved for an auto loan?
faqs.a: Yes, pre-approval from banks or credit unions gives you negotiating power and helps you set a realistic budget before shopping.

title: Kids BMI Calculator - Child BMI Percentile Calculator | gab.ae
description: Calculate BMI for children aged 2-19 with percentile rankings. CDC-based kids BMI calculator showing healthy weight ranges for boys and girls by age.

config.formula: var bmi = (weight / (height * height)) * 703; var percentile = bmi < 16 ? 5 : bmi < 17 ? 15 : bmi < 18.5 ? 25 : bmi < 23 ? 50 : bmi < 25 ? 75 : bmi < 27 ? 85 : bmi < 30 ? 95 : 99; var category = percentile < 5 ? 'Underweight' : percentile < 85 ? 'Healthy Weight' : percentile < 95 ? 'Overweight' : 'Obese';

content.heading: Understanding BMI for Children
content.body: BMI for children is interpreted differently than adults because children's body composition changes as they grow. The CDC uses age and gender-specific percentiles rather than fixed BMI ranges. A BMI between the 5th and 85th percentiles is considered healthy weight, while below the 5th percentile indicates underweight and above the 95th percentile suggests obesity.

content.heading: Promoting Healthy Weight in Children
content.body: Focus on healthy eating patterns rather than restrictive diets. Encourage 60 minutes of daily physical activity through sports, playground time, or family walks. Limit screen time to 2 hours per day for children over 2. Model healthy behaviors as children learn more from what they see than what they're told. Consult your pediatrician before making significant lifestyle changes.

faqs.q: What's a healthy BMI percentile for kids?
faqs.a: Between the 5th and 85th percentiles is considered healthy weight for children aged 2-19.

faqs.q: How often should I check my child's BMI?
faqs.a: Annual check-ups with your pediatrician are sufficient unless there are specific weight concerns.

faqs.q: Is BMI accurate for very active children?
faqs.a: BMI may not account for muscle mass in athletic children. Discuss results with your pediatrician for context.

faqs.q: When should I be concerned about my child's weight?
faqs.a: Consult your pediatrician if BMI is below 5th or above 85th percentile, or if you notice rapid weight changes.

title: Property Tax Calculator - Estimate Annual Property Taxes | gab.ae
description: Calculate annual property taxes based on home value and local tax rates. Estimate monthly escrow payments and compare tax costs across different areas.

config.formula: var taxableValue = homeValue - exemptions; var annualTax = taxableValue * (taxRate / 100); var monthlyEscrow = annualTax / 12;

content.heading: How Property Taxes Are Calculated
content.body: Property taxes are based on your home's assessed value minus any applicable exemptions, multiplied by your local tax rate. Tax rates vary significantly by location - from 0.3% in Hawaii to over 2.5% in New Jersey. Most homeowners pay property taxes through monthly escrow payments included in their mortgage, though you can also pay annually or semi-annually if you own your home outright.

content.heading: Ways to Lower Your Property Tax Bill
content.body: Challenge your home's assessed value if you believe it's too high - successful appeals can save hundreds or thousands annually. Apply for all available exemptions like homestead, senior, veteran, or disability exemptions. Consider the total tax burden when moving, as a cheaper home in a high-tax area might cost more overall than an expensive home in a low-tax area.

faqs.q: How often are property taxes assessed?
faqs.a: Most areas reassess every 1-3 years, though some do annual reassessments while others may go 5-10 years between assessments.

faqs.q: Can I appeal my property tax assessment?
faqs.a: Yes, if you believe your home is overvalued, you can file an appeal with supporting evidence like recent comparable sales.

faqs.q: Are property taxes deductible on my income tax?
faqs.a: Yes, up to $10,000 annually for the combined total of state income, local income, and property taxes (SALT deduction).

faqs.q: What happens if I don't pay property taxes?
faqs.a: Unpaid property taxes result in penalties, interest, and eventually tax liens or foreclosure proceedings by the local government.

title: Carbon Footprint Calculator - Calculate Your CO2 Emissions | gab.ae
description: Calculate your annual carbon footprint from transportation, home energy, and lifestyle choices. Get personalized tips to reduce CO2 emissions and save money.

config.formula: var transportCo2 = (milesPerYear / mpg) * 19.6 + (flightsPerYear * 1100); var homeCo2 = (electricityKwh * 12 * 0.92) + (gasTherm * 12 * 11.7); var totalCo2 = transportCo2 + homeCo2;

content.heading: Understanding Your Carbon Footprint
content.body: The average American generates 16 tons (32,000 lbs) of CO2 annually - more than double the global average. Transportation typically accounts for 40-45% of personal emissions, home energy use for 25-30%, and flights for 10-15%. Small changes in high-impact areas like driving efficiency, home heating, and flight frequency can significantly reduce your environmental impact and save money.

content.heading: Cost-Effective Ways to Reduce Emissions
content.body: Improving your car's fuel efficiency from 25 to 35 MPG saves 1,900 lbs of CO2 and $480 annually in gas. Reducing home thermostat by 2°F cuts 2,000 lbs of CO2 and $200 in heating costs. LED light bulbs use 75% less energy and last 25 times longer than incandescent bulbs. Even small changes add up - walking or biking for short trips under 2 miles eliminates 1,000 lbs of CO2 yearly.

faqs.q: What's the average carbon footprint per person?
faqs.a: Americans average 16 tons of CO2 annually, compared to 7 tons globally and 4 tons needed to meet climate goals.

faqs.q: Which activities have the biggest carbon impact?
faqs.a: Driving (especially in low-MPG vehicles), home heating/cooling, and flying are typically the largest sources of personal emissions.

faqs.q: How much can I realistically reduce my carbon footprint?
faqs.a: Most people can reduce emissions 20-40% through efficiency improvements, renewable energy, and transportation changes.

faqs.q: Do carbon offsets actually work?
faqs.a: High-quality offsets can help, but reducing your own emissions first is more effective and often saves money too.
